video_title: Why AI Agents Need Crypto Rails w/ Arpan Nanavati, CEO of Beep
transcript: Don't fight it. Don't fight AI. Don't fight agents. It's just a losing battle. It's like fighting internet in the 1900s. Human GDP is not going anywhere. We're going to still drink our coffees and eat [music] dinners and go to barber shops, but there's going to be this machine economy that runs in parallel to human GDP. People ask me the same question like why didn't this why now? The answer to that is there's three compounding trends [music] that are converging for the very first time. And for agents to be successful, they're going to need the underlying architecture [music] to translate to their behavior, which is 1,000 transactions in parallel [music] in a second. In my opinion, Sui is the only stack that does that. For any young kids that are just starting their career, just immerse yourself completely [music] in that. Use this as your key growth driver. >> Hi everyone and welcome to Sui Live. We're in Miami and we're joined by Arpan Nanavati, CEO of Beep. Arpan, thank you so much for joining us. How are you? >> Very good. Excited to be here. It's a good time. It's great to be back in Miami. Great to to yeah, hang out with you guys. >> Yeah, absolutely. And this is Sui's first conference in the United States. It's live. How are How are you How are you feeling here at this conference today? >> It's pretty surreal. Last time we were in Singapore and having it back stateside, I think that's just a great momentum shift. You know, even the community is very excited. There's just so many builders we met today. AI is taking shape and blockchain is the perfect recipe for AI to become successful. So, yeah, it's just good times. Good times for anyone to be building in this ecosystem right now. >> Yeah, absolutely. And for our audience, you may remember Arpan from his recent appearance on Journeyman with Raoul where they spoke about all things AI, the GenZ economy, and Beep. And that's what we're going to talk about today because Beep is just basically sitting at front and center of, you know, this industry that we're in and we're hearing about every day, which is AI, right? And you know, I just want to talk about cuz, you know, Beep is building, you know, building out the agentic payment system. And want to ask you, what does what does it mean for AI agents to have payment rails? >> Yeah, so I'm going to take a little step back and give a macro picture, very typical of Roll, but within the tech frame. Uh human GDP today is about 110 trillion dollars, powered by humans primarily and human labor driving economic activity. We're seeing AI taking a lot of the human labor activities, like for example, research or finance or uh you know, there's many other use cases. And what that means is AI is replacing those human jobs, not in a one-to-one fashion, but in a one-to-one thousand fashion. And those 1,000 transactions is a big fire hose of activity, which has economic implications, not just implications, economic returns behind it. You have to pay for tokens. You have to pay to a different service provider. You have to pay to a different agent. So this network effect is having uh a a exponential increase. And what that means is agents need to be able to conduct economic activity with other agents. That is a key requirement. Today's financial system is structured around humans, human identity, human speed, human KYC. Agents don't have KYC. They can't just walk into Wells Fargo or Bank of America and say, "Give me an account so that I can collect payments against it." So agents need a payment standard that is native to agents, which is not native to humans, both in terms of speed, in terms of interaction, and in terms of streaming nature. And that's what we call it as agentic payments. That's one of the first products that we launched. Uh we call it A402, short form for Agent 402. 402 is a payment standard that has existed for two decades, three decades. I come from PayPal, so I've built payments for humans at massive scale, and now we intend to build that for agents, which we believe is going to be 100 times larger than the human economic scale. >> Yeah, and you know, that's one of the things, too, right? That that scale and kind of the speed at which these these agents need to be operating on, and you know, there it's microtransactions basically at that speed, right? And why isn't it essentially talked a little bit about it because you know, basically it's built for, you know, kind of human interaction, right? But why why isn't the infrastructure today ready for kind of that agentic payment system? >> Yeah, I think this is a great question. People ask me the same question, like why didn't this why now? Why didn't happen 5 years ago? Why is it not going to happen 5 years in the future? The answer to that is there's three compounding trends that are converging for the very first time, and that's happening right now, like literally in the last 3 to 6 months that convergence has started happening. What are those three trends? One is tokenized everything, which means bunch of financial assets coming on chain. Number two is permissionless fast blockchain roads, like Sui, for example. And then the third is the cost of entrance. The cost that AI agents still need to consume tokens from an LLM. Those costs have significantly decreased up to 1,000x over the last 2 years to make these agents economically viable to run against your standard human labor activity. So, this is the very first time these three trends are converging, which makes agentic finance, the entire financial category, which includes payments, yield, trading, assets, bonds, etc., all of that accessible at a very low cost. Net net, it's cheap agents, cheap intelligence, and very fast sub-second permissionless blockchain rails, like Sui, make this achievable for agents the very first time and like we're literally living in the present. It's not even a future thing. It's not even a past thing. So like we're literally drinking from the fire hose right now. >> Yeah, there are literally people operating with agents right now as we speak. I was just speaking with Rael. He He has an agent of his own. I have a few agents of my own and they're out and running in the real world. So like you said, it is happening right now. And And you touched on Sui, right? And I think that's what we've been hearing this today and you know, that's what Sui is known for is you know, kind of that speed and and cost of of of transactions. So and that and it seems like that makes AI agents and that the payment system a natural fit. Is that why you decided to build on Sui or was there something more behind it? >> Yeah, so I'll answer that question. Before that, the street cred in SF is the number of agents you have. So you know, you walk over walk around and and hang out with people like the number one question is how many agents are you running? So just sorry for that type side [laughter] type it. But coming back to your question, why Sui? Uh Sui's architecture is very elegantly designed around object model and parallel execution of that object model. Agents' behavior is very different from a human behavior. Like our Our human behavior is me We me we do one transaction, two transactions. If you're feeling degen, we'll do 10 transactions a day. Agents do 1,000 transactions in a second. So you need a system or an L1 system that can encapsulate one transaction of that agent in a particular object which is composable and only Sui has that. Uh it's not batch transactions like other L2s have. It's not sequential, but it's parallel. You need these 1,000 transactions to be executed in parallel so that the settlement happens within a second. I think Sui's architecture is the only one that I know of in the current world that can support this type of agent behavior. Uh and for agents to be successful, they're going to need the underlying architecture to translate to their behavior, which is 1,000 transactions in parallel in a second, in my opinion. So, this is the only stack that does that. >> Now, we we can we get excited with what our agents can do with payments and, you know, just have the the ease of which they can operate, but what are What do you think are some of maybe the biggest difficulties right now facing kind of agentic payments? >> Yeah, I think so. Uh one of the key things that even we're building, and this is a problem, is yeah, you can have an agent, but you can't give a wallet to it because our wallets today are still built around human behavior. You need a transaction to sign. It's still slow. You configure slippage, risk tolerance, signing keys. All of that stuff is still manual. So, for agent to conduct any financial activity, it needs a wallet infrastructure, which is parallel execution as one. Number two is automatic signing. And number three is composable key management so that the human does not Human cannot fathom to approve 1,000 transactions or sign 1,000 transactions on your wallet in 1 second. So, that's where BEAP is also building uh is enabling agents to seamlessly transact with the wallet infrastructure, which is native to all blockchains. And that is needed. So, if you just go to Claude today or GPT and say, "Hey, here's my slush or here's my Phantom, do things with it." Like, it's going to be very hard for that agent to do anything with it. >> Now, you know, another thing and we we did touch on that that people are using this right now. Your street credit is how many agents you have up and running. Um but like in terms of kind of the the agent economy development now, right? And I do I think it's more so for people that maybe not operating with agents, maybe not seeing essentially how fast this is going. Like, where do you see us right now in that development? Is it Do you see it more like kind of that 1995 like AOL type moment where everyone like started getting emails right away or are we further are we actually like further along than that? >> We're much further along and we're much faster along versus the 1995 curve or any other cycle curve. Um and what why I say that is agents the economy factor or the aspect of agents is near zero today compared to human GDP. So like that's that's the honest fact. Why is that? Because they were locked out of the financial system. They couldn't get KYC. This is the very first time like I said in the present that agents have viable cost factor as well as the execution factor where agents can create economic activity. So we're seeing it grow very fast and I think where this goes is within a year or so we'll see an agent economy or agentic economy or MGDP is what I call it for machine GDP be a trillion dollars even before we have a name for it. It's going to grow super fast given the fact that these three things have converged which has unlocked agentic finance as a category and now agents are not locked out of the financial system as they were. So yeah, we will we'll see the network effects. Metcalfe's law will come in and every dollar of AI cost reduced will cause 100 new agents to come online and those 100 agents are going to do economic activity. So you're going to see an exponential higher network effect of agents and exponentially higher effect of machine GDP coming online. It's not a zero-sum game. Human GDP is not going anywhere. We're going to still drink our coffees and eat dinners and go to barber shops, but there's going to be this machine economy that runs in parallel to human GDP and hopefully it supersedes the human GDP given the network effects that are play in play right now. So I want to talk to you about some use cases that we're we're seeing, you know, in kind of in today with agents and the the agentic economy. Um in your in your view, I guess for the individual user, what is what do you see as kind of like right now as being a a very valuable use case that they can use today? And then on the other side of that, maybe for like an institution, what do you see as like a powerful use case for them? >> Yeah, so our lines are the institutions I first we just launched today our tokenized strategies product, which means a fund could come in, create an AI strategy, AI yield strategy, tokenize it, put it on chain, and that means the flexibility of the supply, the entry of barriers very low, and then the demand side increases because now that fund in a traditional sense is tokenized, it's an agentic capital market, or it's an agentic fund now, which has access to the entire planet now. So, both demand side and supply side is going to get unlocked very quickly because of this institutional product uh that we just launched today on stage. Uh the second point is what are some of the viable use cases? Um I think what I'll There's like a wide range of use cases. What I'll say is like some of the use cases that I use on a daily basis, you know, given the geopolitical stuff that's happening everywhere, I want to trade that. Uh so, I my agent on Beep will create a custom commodities index, as well as create a custom index on Polymarket and trade that even at 3:00 a.m. while I'm sleeping. And if there's some news happening on that side there's going to be a trade that's going to be made. Like it's impossible for me as a human to be able to trade that market. So, it that's one use case. Another use case is in the traditional equity sense, I also have equities which are now on chain, and because of these geopolitical events, that agent could also rebalance my portfolio against the traditional equity sense. Just based on intent, now you have agents that are driving these trading yield behaviors for you. Uh that's like super exciting, you know, it's a massive unlock for people that traditionally would have to go to a financial advisor, explain what they wanted them, and by the time they're done explaining, the trade's gone. So, in this case, like within 3 minutes, they're enabling their intent and trading against it. Another use case is there's a lot of research agents that are coming online. >> [snorts] >> Um, we were just talking to one of the media houses, I won't name them. Uh, they're saying bots are coming, taking our content, not paying us. And the simple explanation to their use case is put a paywall. Put an agentic paywall. You have an agent on the demand side that's trying to consume your content. Charge them per paragraph or charge them per piece of text to 1 cent, and then the agent will be able to pay, so you're monetizing it, and then the cost is so low that it's still economically viable for the buyer agent to pay you 1 cent or 10 cents for a piece of content. So, I think that's a very massive media use case that is coming online right now. >> Oh, wow. Yeah, those are the >> For agentic payments, yeah. >> Love to hear the use cases cuz I think it's valuable for people to also understand like things that they can do with it cuz, you know, sometimes they might not be aware. So, thought that was great. And then now I want to ask you cuz we we see so many like, you know, with AI agents, we see so many iterations of them, right? Hermes is is in the news now, right? Open Claw had its run, and I mean, still does, right? And then you you talked about the LLMs with the the Claudes and the Chat GPTs, Geminis of the world. How do you see the agents kind of operating and interacting with them? Do you see it kind of like a multi-model type of system? Like how do you view how the agents are going to choose which to use or are like is the operator going to kind of pick that? How how do you think about all of that? >> Uh, agent in a simplistic sense is a wrapper on top of LLM. Which means any of these harnesses should be able interact with an agent that is on a different harness. Um and there's a standard called as MCP, which enables agent-to-agent communication. Uh not just payments, but just hey, can I use you? What data do you have? What can I talk to you? What is your service? So that MCP interface is the is the standard that allows an agent to talk to another agent. But agent, simply speaking, it you know, I'm oversimplifying it, but in simple terms, explaining to a five-year-old, it's a wrapper on top of a model. AI model. So it should, in theory and in practice, it's still uh you know, you can talk to any agent. Uh it could be a deep seek agent, it could be a Claude agent, it could be a GPT agent, it doesn't matter. MCP is the common interface between all of that. >> Mhm. Yeah, and it's funny that you mentioned that cuz I think, you know, in the future we're going to see kind of new job roles coming up with, you know, it's going to be an agent operator or, you know, a prompt engineer, and those and having an MCP scale and knowing all the CLIs is going to be extremely important skills for to get those new jobs. So that's just another development of how the agentic economy won't just be helping itself, but also actually be helping humans who who can, you know, kind of adapt to it as well. Um and then kind of speaking of that, what do you what does the mainstream agentic commerce look like to you in 3 years? >> I think it's a it's a great question and something that we've dabbled with as well. Uh candidly, I don't know the answer where this goes. Uh what I know is the trends are leading to a fact that your standard economy, like including your DoorDash to all the others use cases that we talked through, like there's no reason like it's just a better business model for agents to be running that. Uh in my opinion, every human's going to have 100 to 1,000 agents uh that will either order food for them or read news content or do some financial activities or do some sales or marketing or customer support or fight charges against them or do some legal stuff. It's inevitable in my opinion. I think the question is when. The question is not if. I think it's inevitable. It's going to happen. Like my previous company was in the AI legal space. We were building legal agents. Two years ago it just felt so far-fetched. Now it's just a common standard. >> Yeah. And you mentioned actually you've been basically working with AI before you even really, you know, basically before AI was even really a thing that was being talked about, right? So it's just so crazy and that wasn't even that long ago. So it's just crazy how fast it's actually coming. So I love that you said it's just a when and that when is going to be sooner than we realize. >> Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. The the compounding trends are are really cracking it. And the accessibility is really high. So the demand side is just it's a firehose that is coming in and now the firehose has a rails to run on. So it just works. >> I want to leave you, you know, with with kind of the last word here. For someone who's watching this conversation and they see AI in the news, maybe they might be overwhelmed because like so much, you know, new developments are coming out, but they want to learn about it and you know, what what's kind of your What do you want to leave them with about kind of the the era and the opportunity that we have in front of us with this? >> Don't fight it. Don't fight AI. Don't fight agents. It's just a losing battle. It's like fighting internet in the 1900s. Like the results are in front of us. So that's kind of the first thing to say and where to start just immerse yourself. As simple as go to GPT and start doing things that you would call people and ask or what you would Google search for and then start layering up from there to then managing certain services or your skills to then if you're a business small business owner start setting up a customer service pipeline through agents that are talking on your behalf or doing some sales stuff to then you know, as you get more and more comfortable, uh you can get into financial activity with that as well. Um but yeah, I I this is what I keep telling everyone, don't fight it. Uh I'm even having my parents >> [laughter] >> uh work with AI uh begrudgingly. You know, they love it cuz there's a lot of questions that they would ask me and I'm like, I can answer it, but try it with GPT, you'll you'll self-service uh get your answer. So, I think it's it's coming both in the physical and the digital world. Like, we see it in the physical world with cars. Why I say this is, you know, my parents are aging, so I would want them to be in a car that's driven by AI, not driven by them. Uh so, yeah, I think it's it's a it's a great time uh for all of us and for any young kids uh that are just starting their career, just immerse yourself completely in that. Uh use this as your um as your as your key growth driver. >> You know, Arpan, it's been a pleasure speaking with you here. We're definitely excited for what Beep is building. It's it's it's new, it's innovative, it's fun, so we'll be following along the journey and we can't see wait to see any new developments you have coming. >> Awesome. Thank you so much. This is great chatting. I've been a part of our way community for many, many years, so uh this is exciting to help the our way community as well and share some of our thoughts here. All right. >> Thank you.
